name: Game scripts · Metin2 Python native build helpers
description: Small toolchain for compiling Python extension modules into native binaries for legacy Metin2 workflows—narrow C-extension boundary, reproducible build steps, and explicit trust posture around compiled artifacts.
longDescription: Small toolchain for building C extensions with `setupdll.py` and `build_ext`, producing Windows-native binaries (`.pyd` outputs renamed to `.dll` where required by the client). Scope stays narrow: native helpers alongside client-side automation, not a full game engine—keeping the attack surface and maintenance burden bounded. Uses a legacy Python 2.7 / 32-bit-oriented toolchain; automation should follow the game publisher’s terms of service. Native code demands clear provenance: document compiler versions and avoid distributing unsigned binaries as trusted software.
features: Documented build_ext flow for in-place native modules
Guidance on renaming outputs for client-side loading expectations
Minimal surface area for maintainers and security reviewers
Explicit toolchain pinning to reproduce bytecode/native artefacts across machines
role: Native tooling for game automation experiments
highlights: Demonstrates low-level Windows native build steps for game client extensions
challenges: Legacy Python stacks are difficult to reproduce on current workstations.
solutions: Document the expected interpreter and keep build scripts isolated from unrelated dependencies.
security: Never distribute unsigned binaries as trusted software—code signing where appropriate
Native extensions are high-risk supply-chain targets; verify compiler and dependency provenance
Assume game automation may violate ToS—use only where permitted
Isolate build environments from production credentials
